topic: End-to-end household finance planner with common pool income and expense management
questionsAsked: Who is this for — just your own household, or SaaS / open-source?
Do you want automatic bank sync (Plaid) or manual entry/CSV for MVP?
What's your preferred database and hosting approach?
What's the most important first capability?
constraints: Single household only — no multi-tenant, no billing
Manual entry + CSV import only (no bank API for MVP)
All core features needed: budget, expenses, bills, debt, savings
Encrypt sensitive fields, RLS on all tables

candidates.name: Monolith Dashboard
candidates.summary: Full feature set as pages within the existing Next.js app. Supabase handles auth, DB, RLS. Server Components for reads, Server Actions for writes.
risks: Scope creep — many features. Mitigate with phased delivery.
CSV import complexity varies by bank. Mitigate with configurable mapper.
mvp: Account and transaction CRUD with categories
Dashboard with monthly overview and trends

candidates.name: Local-First Hybrid
candidates.summary: SQLite in browser (wa-sqlite/OPFS) for instant local reads/writes, syncing to Supabase Postgres for backup and cross-device access.
risks: Sync complexity — conflict resolution for shared household edits
Browser storage limits and OPFS compatibility issues
mvp: Same features as Option A plus sync layer

candidates.name: API-First with Mobile Future
candidates.summary: Separate tRPC/REST API layer on Supabase, Next.js as one client. Designed for future React Native mobile app.
risks: YAGNI — mobile app may never materialize
Slower iteration due to extra abstraction
mvp: Same features as Option A but with API abstraction layer
Shared type package between API and frontend

recommendation.rationale: For a single-household private tool, simplicity wins. Next.js Server Components + Supabase gives auth, RLS, realtime, and Postgres with zero infra management. Ships faster, iterates faster. Architecture naturally supports adding Plaid or mobile later without rewrite.
